content.problemStatement:
Alice and Bob are playing a game. They have two positive integers XXX and YYY.

In one operation:


First Alice would replace **exactly one** of the numbers with the gcd of current numbers. In words, she would select either XXX or YYY and replace it with gcd⁡(X,Y)\gcd(X,Y)gcd(X,Y).
Then Bob would replace **exactly one** of the numbers with the lcm of current numbers.
Find the **minimum sum** of numbers they can achieve after KKK number of operations.


Input Format

The first line of input will contain a single integer TTT, denoting the number of test cases.
Each test case consists of three space-separated integers X,Y,X, Y,X,Y, and KKK — denoting the initial numbers and the number of operations.


Output Format
For each test case, output on a new line, the **minimum sum** of numbers they can achieve after KKK operations.
content.inputFormat: The first line contains an integer T, the number of test cases. Each of the next T lines contains three space‑separated integers X, Y and K.
content.outputFormat: For each test case output a single line containing the minimum possible sum of the two numbers after exactly K operations.

solution.approach:
Let g = gcd(X, Y). In one operation Alice can replace one number with g and Bob will then replace one number with the lcm of the current pair. Because g divides the other number, the lcm equals that other number, so Bob will never increase the sum. Hence each operation can only turn one of the numbers into g while leaving the other unchanged. After the first operation the pair becomes (g, min(X, Y)). A second operation can turn the remaining larger number into g as well, after which both numbers are equal to g and further operations have no effect. Therefore:
- If K == 1, the optimal sum is g + min(X, Y).
- If K >= 2, the optimal sum is 2 * g.
The algorithm is O(1) per test case using the Euclidean algorithm for gcd.
solution.timeComplexity: O(T * log(min(X, Y))) – gcd computation per test case.
